Splash Sports · CS Training Guide

NFL Pick'em

A complete walkthrough of the commissioner and player experience — contest creation, every settings tab, making picks, and managing entrants.

Contents
IntroCreating a ContestThe Contest Home ScreenCommissioner DashboardSettings — GeneralSettings — Format & SpreadsSettings — ScoringSettings — PicksheetSettings — SegmentsSettings — TiebreakersSettings — Entrant GroupsSettings — DesignThe Player ExperienceLeaderboardsAuto-Picks Quick ReferenceEntrants ManagerInvites ManagerFAQ & Glossary
01

What Is NFL Pick'em?

NFL Pick'em is a multi-week, private, invite-only contest. Each week, players pick winners — and optionally point totals (over/unders) — for the games on that week's slate. It's one flexible product that a commissioner configures to fit their group, alongside Survivor and combined NFL + NCAAF variants.

Where it lives

From "Choose a contest format," Pick'em sits in its own tab alongside Survivor and Other formats. NFL Pick'em is the single-league version — there's also an NFL + NCAAF combined Pick'em, plus Confidence variants of both.

The four Pick'em-family formats: NFL + NCAAF Pick'Em, NFL Pick'em, NFL Confidence Pick'em, and NFL + NCAAF Confidence Pick'em.

This guide walks through NFL Pick'em specifically: straight-up or against-the-spread picks, one game slate per week, for the full regular season (or whatever week range the commissioner sets).

The three building blocks every commissioner chooses

FormatStandard (straightforward win/loss scoring) or Confidence (entrants rank their picks for variable points). "Best Pick" isn't a separate Format — it's a bonus feature a commissioner can switch on inside a Standard-format contest. Confidence and Best Pick can never be combined.
Point Spread ModeStraight Up (only cares who wins), or Against the Spread — either Splash's live odds or numbers the commissioner sets by hand.
League / Sport ModeFor this guide, NFL only. (Splash also supports College-only or NFL + College combined contests, covered in a separate guide.)

On top of those three choices, commissioners can layer in Segments, Groups, tiebreakers, weighted scoring, drop-worst-week, and more — all covered below.

Quick facts to lead with

Invite-only — there's no public entry mechanic built into this format itself. One entrant can hold up to 250 entries per contest if the commissioner raises the cap (default is 1). Supports a full NFL season, Week 1 through the Super Bowl. Leaderboards exist at three levels — season-long, weekly, and a "picks by week" grid — plus optional Segment and Group sub-leaderboards. Nearly every commissioner setting can be changed mid-season, with clear rules for what happens to already-graded weeks.

Quick Check

Which of the three building blocks is 'Standard vs. Confidence' part of?

Is 'Best Pick' a separate Format option?

What's the maximum number of entries one person can hold per contest?

02

Creating a Contest

Once a commissioner selects NFL Pick'em, the last step of setup is naming the contest.

The name field defaults to a placeholder of "NFL Pick'em" — commissioners can personalize it (e.g. "Abbey's NFL Pick'em Test"). Clicking Create contest finalizes setup and drops the commissioner into their new contest.

Service fee heads-up

A one-time service fee applies when the contest starts. Commissioners can waive it by setting up Contest Vault (Splash's optional payment-collection tool, shared across all contest formats) — otherwise they can manage entry fees themselves outside of Splash.

Quick Check

What can a commissioner set up to waive the one-time service fee?

03

The Contest Home Screen

Every contest — for both players and commissioners — is built around the same left-hand navigation panel. It always shows the entries widget at top (how many entries are filled vs. the max, plus a lock countdown), followed by player-facing tabs, and, for commissioners only, a separate Commissioner section below a divider.

Player-facing tabs:

  • My entries — where an entrant makes and reviews their picks.
  • Activity — a chronological log of commissioner changes: settings updates, group moves, and pick edits made on an entrant's behalf.
  • Contest details — the read-only rules summary for the contest.

Commissioner-only tabs (below the divider):

  • Dashboard — the getting-started/setup hub.
  • Contest settings — every rule and format toggle (covered in detail below).
  • Entrants manager — the full entrant roster, pick logs, and emailing tools.
  • Invites manager — sharing/inviting tools.
  • Contest Vault — optional payment collection (shared across formats, not covered in depth here).

The main contest view. The black card on the left always shows the lock countdown and entries filled/max. On the right, each of the commissioner's own entries appears as a card with pick slots (one per team on the week's slate) and quick links to Withdraw or Make picks.

Until a commissioner edits a setting or a pick on someone's behalf, the Activity tab simply shows "No activity yet." Once changes start happening, they're logged here chronologically, split between general Activity and a dedicated Pick changes filter.

Quick Check

Which tab shows a chronological log of commissioner-made changes to picks and settings?

Which section of the sidebar is only visible to the commissioner?

04

Commissioner Dashboard & Getting Started

The Dashboard tab is the commissioner's landing page right after creating a contest. It tracks setup progress with a simple checklist and surfaces the contest's key numbers at a glance.

The three top counters — Entries available, Invites sent, and Entered — update live as the commissioner invites people and they join.

Below the counters is a numbered setup checklist:

  1. Contest created — automatically checked off.
  2. Set up entry fee collection — choose Set up Contest Vault (to waive the service fee and collect/distribute payments online across all 50 states + Canada) or I'll manage payments myself.
  3. Review contest settings — a reminder to fine-tune rules, entries, lock times, and other details before players join, with a shortcut into Review settings.
Defaults work out of the box

Splash pre-fills sensible defaults for every setting so a contest is playable immediately after creation — the Dashboard's "want to fine-tune anything?" prompt is optional, not required, before inviting players.

Quick Check

Which of these is NOT one of the three live counters on the Dashboard?

What does 'Set up Contest Vault' let a commissioner do?

05

Contest Settings: General Tab

The General tab covers the contest's identity and its high-level schedule and entry rules.

Commissioners can rename the contest at any time and add an optional description (up to 500 characters) that entrants will see.

  • Start week / End week — defines the first and last week of the contest (e.g., Week 1 through Week 18 for a full regular season).
  • Maximum entries per entrant — how many entries one person can hold, from 1 to 250.
  • Entry deadline — the date/time entries close. Toggle on to set a custom deadline instead of the default.
  • Lock entries immediately — stops new members from joining right away, regardless of the entry deadline.
  • Hide Future Picks From Commissioner — controls whether the commissioner can see and edit members' future (not-yet-locked) picks. This is on by default, which keeps commissioners honest in contests where they're also playing.

A live "Settings preview" panel recaps every choice made across all settings tabs — contest type, format, entries, schedule, and scoring — so commissioners can sanity-check everything in one place before saving.

Entry rules — what CS should know

Entry DeadlineOff by default. When turned on, defaults to Sunday 1pm ET of the contest's starting week, but can be moved any time up until the last week of the contest starts. No new entries are accepted once it passes.
Prevent New EntriesA hard stop that immediately closes the contest to new players. Turns on automatically once the Entry Deadline passes, but a commissioner can also flip it on manually at any time.
Maximum entries per entrantDefaults to 1; can be raised to any whole number up to 250 — but once the contest is live, it can only be raised, never lowered.
Joining mid-seasonA late joiner is scored 0-for-every-required-pick on all weeks that already completed before they joined. They compete normally starting with the next open week.
Joining after the contest endsNot allowed — no new entries can be accepted once the contest has ended.
Quick Check

What does 'Hide Future Picks From Commissioner' control?

What's the valid range for Maximum entries per entrant?

Once a contest is live, can Maximum entries per entrant be lowered?

If someone joins mid-season, how are the weeks before they joined scored?

06

Contest Settings: Format & Spreads Tab

This tab controls the single most important scoring decision in Pick'em: whether entrants pick straight-up winners or against-the-spread covers.

  • Straight up — entrants pick the team they think will win outright. No spreads involved at all (though a commissioner can still toggle on Display spreads on picksheet for informational purposes only).
  • Against the spread (Splash-set) — entrants pick teams to cover a point spread that Splash sets and updates automatically.
  • Against the spread (Commissioner-set) — same concept, but the commissioner enters and manages the spread values manually.

Choosing either "Against the spread" option reveals a shared set of additional settings:

  • Spread lock time — Splash spreads update dynamically through the week; this sets when they stop updating and lock in for grading (e.g., Tuesday 11:00 AM). Once that time passes for a given week, it can't be edited.
  • Picks availability — Allow Picks in Advance (entrants can pick any future week) vs. Do not allow picks until spreads are set (entrants can only pick once the prior slate finishes and new spreads are ready).
  • Spread type — Hook (half-point lines that avoid pushes entirely) vs. Push (whole-number lines where ties/pushes are possible and need a scoring rule).
  • Push scoring (only shown when Spread type = Push) — a pushed pick can Count as win, Count as loss, or receive Half point credit.
Commissioner-set spreads

With the Commissioner-set option, spreads are entered from the home team's perspective: use a negative number if the home team is favored, or a positive number if the home team is the underdog. The Manage spreads button opens a dedicated spreads editor per game — but the contest must be saved first before that editor becomes available.

Defaults & edge cases CS should know

Hooked Spreads defaultOn by default for Splash-set ATS — the half-point is already there to prevent pushes unless a commissioner turns it off.
Spread lock vs. pick deadlineIf the spread lock time is set later than the pick deadline, spreads simply lock at the pick deadline instead. If it's set after a given game already started, that game's spread locks at kickoff.
Totals lock timingIn Splash-set ATS contests, totals (over/unders) lock on the same schedule as spreads. In Straight Up contests, totals lock by default on Tuesdays at 12pm ET.
Editing mid-seasonChanging Point Spread Mode or Hooked Spreads mid-season re-grades any already-completed weeks and emails affected entrants.
Quick Check

Which scoring mode has entrants picking teams to win outright, with no spread involved?

With Commissioner-set spreads, what does a negative number mean for the home team?

Is Hooked Spreads on or off by default for Splash-set ATS contests?

If the spread lock time is set later than the pick deadline, when do spreads actually lock?

What happens if a commissioner changes Point Spread Mode mid-season?

07

Contest Settings: Scoring Tab

The Scoring tab lets commissioners amplify certain weeks and forgive bad ones.

  • Weighted scoring — applies a per-week point multiplier. Use 1x for weeks that keep the default value; bump later weeks up (e.g., 2x for Week 16, 2x for Week 17, 4x for Week 18) to make the stretch run matter more.
  • Drop worst week — lets each entry automatically drop its lowest-scoring week(s) from the season total.
    • Weeks to drop — how many of an entry's lowest-scoring weeks get excluded (e.g., "1 lowest-scoring week").
    • Last drop week — the last week in the contest that's eligible to be dropped (or "No limit").
Why commissioners use this

Weighting late-season weeks keeps the race alive into the fantasy-playoff stretch, and dropping a bad week softens the blow of a rough bye-week slate or a week someone missed.

Additional details CS should know

  • Individual game point values — beyond the weekly multiplier, each individual game can also be assigned its own "Point Value" from 1–20 (set in the Picksheet tab's weekly customization, covered next). A Week 1 game worth 2 points becomes 4 points under a 2× week multiplier.
  • Drop Worst Week can go up to ten — a commissioner isn't limited to dropping just one week; they can configure the contest to drop up to a player's worst ten weeks.
  • Retroactive edits — Weighted Scoring can even be edited for weeks that already happened. Doing so triggers a re-grade and emails affected entrants.
Quick Check

What multiplier keeps a week's default scoring value unchanged?

'Last drop week' controls...

What's the maximum number of worst weeks a commissioner can configure Drop Worst Week to exclude?

What happens if a commissioner edits Weighted Scoring for a week that's already been played?

08

Contest Settings: Picksheet Tab

The Picksheet tab controls pick requirements, autopick behavior, and what actually shows up on the weekly picksheet.

  • Pick deadline — when the weekly picksheet locks (defaults to Sundays at 12:00 PM CDT).
  • Picks per week — how many games players must pick each week (e.g., "Pick all games").
  • Enable Best Picks Per Week — lets players designate certain picks as their "Best Picks" for bonus points if correct. Commissioners set both the required number of Best Picks per week and the bonus points awarded for each correct one.

Autopick automatically fills in picks for anyone who misses the weekly deadline.

  • Who sets the auto-pick setting? — either Commissioner sets for all (one rule for everyone) or Each player chooses (players pick their own auto-pick preference when they join).
  • Team pick logic — how missed team picks are auto-filled. Options: All Home Teams, All Away Teams, All Favorites, All Underdogs, or Random.
  • Over/Under logic — how missed totals picks are auto-filled. Options: All Overs, All Unders, or Random.
  • Maximum amount of auto picks — defaults to unlimited; a commissioner can cap it at any whole number. Once the cap is hit, further missed picks grade as losses instead of being auto-filled.
  • Auto Pick Forgiveness — the number of picks (0–10) an entrant can miss in a single week before it even counts as an "autopick week" toward that cap. Defaults to 0.
Autopick priority rule

Autopick only fills games not already picked by the deadline. If a game's winner slot and its Over/Under slot both compete for the last available pick, the winner pick takes priority.

Under Picksheet Setup, commissioners choose which leagues/conferences appear on the picksheet (with Over/Under totals toggled independently per league) and which days of the week are eligible for games to appear. Season defaults apply contest-wide, while Weekly customizations let a commissioner override those defaults for an individual week — including per-game Point Values (1–20) and marking specific games as "Must Pick" for that week.

Pick deadline options

Beyond the default day/time, a commissioner can instead set the Pick deadline to first game time each week, last game time each week, or a fully custom deadline (any day, any time). Whichever is chosen, it stays editable for any future week — only weeks whose deadline has already passed are locked in.

Quick Check

If a player misses the deadline and Autopick's Team pick logic is 'All home teams,' what happens?

Where would a commissioner override picksheet settings for just one specific week?

What does 'Maximum amount of auto picks' do once the cap is reached?

What does Auto Pick Forgiveness let an entrant do?

If a game's winner pick and O/U pick both compete for the last open autopick slot, which wins?

09

Contest Settings: Segments Tab

Segment groups let a commissioner group weeks into named leaderboard segments — for example, splitting the season into "First Half" and "Second Half" leaderboards, or aligning segments to fantasy football's regular season vs. playoffs.

Each segment needs a name plus a start week and end week. Splash requires at least two segments if the feature is used at all — a single segment isn't allowed, since the point is to compare performance across multiple slices of the season.

Quick Check

What is the minimum number of segments a commissioner must add if using this feature?

Segment groups are used to...

10

Contest Settings: Tiebreakers Tab

Ties happen constantly in Pick'em — this tab controls exactly how they're broken, both week-to-week and for the season overall.

Both the Weekly and Season leaderboard tiebreakers work the same way: an ordered list where the first item is highest priority. In the example shown, ties are broken first by Points, then Wins, then Tie Game Points.

  • Use the up/down arrows to reorder priority, or the x to remove a tiebreaker entirely.
  • Add more from the options underneath: Tie Game Pick, Best Pick Wins, # of Auto Picks Used, Win %, and Win Margin.
  • Both lists are editable at any time, even mid-contest.

Separately, commissioners can turn on a classic weekly tiebreaker: entrants predict the total combined points scored in one designated game each week. If turned off, that prediction question disappears — but the leaderboard tiebreaker order above still applies. When it's on, the commissioner picks which specific game serves as that week's tiebreaker game from the full slate, and can flip through weeks using the cadence selector.

Two things worth knowing

Points can never be removed from either tiebreaker cascade — it's always active as the base sort. And Over/Under picks factor into Win Margin too: picking the over on a 42-point total that lands at 52 adds +10 to that entrant's Win Margin, the same way beating a spread or winning outright would.

Quick Check

In the tiebreaker order list, which item has the highest priority?

Turning off the weekly tiebreaker question does what to the leaderboard tiebreaker order?

What does the weekly tiebreaker question ask entrants to predict?

Can 'Points' ever be removed from the tiebreaker cascade?

11

Contest Settings: Entrant Groups Tab

Group standings let a commissioner run sub-leaderboards inside one larger contest — useful anytime entrants naturally split into teams.

  • Commissioner assigns groups — entrants join without choosing; the commissioner sorts everyone afterward.
  • Entrants may choose a group — entrants can optionally pick a group when they join.
  • Entrants must choose a group — entrants are required to select an existing group before they can join at all.
Why this matters for office pools

For a workplace contest, Entrant Groups turn one big pool into a friendly inter-department competition — a "Marketing" group vs. a "Product" group, for example — each with its own group standings layered on top of the overall leaderboard. It's an easy way to add a second layer of bragging rights (department vs. department) without running two separate contests, and it also gives HR/team-building organizers a built-in reason for people across teams to check in weekly.

Quick Check

Which group assignment mode requires entrants to select a group before they can join?

Why might a commissioner enable Entrant Groups for an office contest?

12

Contest Settings: Design Tab

The Design tab is short and sweet — it's where a commissioner uploads a custom contest banner.

Banners accept PNG or JPG files up to 4 MB, and give a contest a personalized look — a company logo for an office pool, a group photo, or custom artwork.

13

Making Picks: The Player Experience

Every entrant's picks live under My entries, which shows one card per entry.

  • Click the pencil icon next to the entry name to rename it.
  • Click Withdraw (top right, in red) to remove the entry from the contest.
  • Click Make picks to open the picksheet for that entry.
Withdrawing is permanent

The confirmation modal is explicit: "This entry will be removed from the contest. You cannot undo this action." A withdrawn entry can never be re-added.

Clicking Make picks opens the full picksheet, organized by week tabs across the top (the current/selected week is highlighted) with the pick deadline and lock status shown just below.

Every game on the slate is shown as a pair of teams with a radio button next to each — click the team an entrant thinks will win (or cover, in an against-the-spread contest) to make that pick.

A running progress tracker at the bottom shows exactly how many picks have been made out of the total required for that week — and a row of small circles fills in with a checkmark for each completed pick.

Always hit Save picks

Picks are not registered until the entrant clicks Save picks. After saving, clicking Edit again reopens the picksheet to continue making picks for other weeks.

Once picks are saved, the entry card updates to show each pick as a team logo and abbreviation (e.g., NE, SF, NYJ, CHI...) instead of an empty slot — with the Edit link replacing "Make picks" so the entrant can jump back in.

The "Rules" link — a great ticket-reducer

At the top of the picksheet, entrants see the pick deadline, the spread lock time (in ATS contests), and a Rules link that opens a modal explaining exactly how that specific week's contest is scored. It only shows the settings actually turned on — so a Straight Up contest with no Best Picks won't mention spreads or bonus points at all. It covers game type, Over/Under scoring, Best Picks, any minimum-pick requirements, any Commissioner Required (Must Pick) games, how pushes are scored, what happens to missed picks and losses, and the tiebreaker (if enabled).

Fast Picks — one-click auto-fill

Entrants can auto-fill their entire picksheet with one click using Fast Picks:

  • Winners — All Home, All Away, All Favorites, All Underdogs, or Random.
  • Over/Unders — All Overs, All Unders, or Random.

If fewer than all picks are required for the week, Fast Picks fills the first eligible games listed. Any Must Pick or minimum-pick requirement a commissioner has set always takes priority over Fast Pick logic.

Quick Check

How does an entrant rename one of their entries?

What must an entrant do for their picks to actually count?

Is withdrawing an entry reversible?

What makes the Rules modal especially useful for reducing support tickets?

If a commissioner has a Must Pick game set for the week, what happens when an entrant uses Fast Picks?

14

Leaderboards

There are three leaderboard views — Season-Long, Weekly, and Picks by Week — plus Segment and Group variations layered on top. All views share the same filters: Search Entrants, Following, My Entries, and CSV Export, plus an info icon explaining every column.

Season-Long Leaderboard

PtsTotal points earned.
W-L-TCumulative wins, losses, and ties across all scored picks this season.
Win%Wins divided by total graded picks.
MarginCumulative margin — margin of victory/defeat (straight up), cover/miss amount (ATS), or +/– the O/U line (totals).
TieDiffTiebreaker differential — absolute difference between predicted and actual score. Lower is better; highlighted green at ≤5.
TG PickWin-loss-tie record specifically on the designated tiebreaker game each week.
BPWBest Pick Wins — number of weeks the entrant's Best Pick was correct.
AutoNumber of weeks with enough auto-picks applied to count as an "autopick week."

Clicking any entrant expands a week-by-week breakdown. A week matching the entrant's dropped week shows an orange background, a strikethrough label, and a DROPPED badge, and is excluded from the season total.

Segment & Group views

Segment view uses the identical layout, just scoped to the weeks in that segment — segment cards display the segment name, included weeks, and week count.

Group view attributes the same stats to the group instead of an individual. Clicking a group row expands an inline mini-leaderboard of that group's members (sorted by points), with the viewer's own entries highlighted and a "YOUR GROUP" badge. Clicking a member row goes one level deeper into their own week-by-week breakdown — a three-level hierarchy: Group → Members → Weeks.

Weekly Leaderboard

PtsPoints earned this week.
Pot. PtsPotential points — the max this entrant can still finish with, based on picks already made plus pending games.
W-L-TThis week's graded record.
MarginSame margin logic as the season view, scoped to this week.
Win%This week's win rate.
TieDiff (Guess)Differential from the tiebreaker guess (guess shown in parentheses).
TG PickResult on this week's designated tiebreaker game.
BPWWhether this week's Best Pick hit (1) or missed (0).

Expanding a row shows every pick made that week: result, matchup, the pick made (with spread or O/U value as applicable), final score, margin, and points earned.

Badges to know

A / AutoPick was auto-assigned because the entrant missed the deadline.
DroppedThis week was excluded from the season total via Drop Worst Week.
BestThe entrant's designated Best Pick for the week.
TBThe tiebreaker game.
MGRPick was entered by the commissioner on the entrant's behalf (hover shows the timestamp).

Picks by Week (grid view)

The Entrant column shows the player's name, weekly points, and tiebreaker guess with differential. Every other column is one game — shaded green for correct, red for wrong, yellow for pending, with a colored border only (no shading) while the game is live. BEST, AUTO, TB, and MGR badges work the same as on the Weekly Leaderboard. Matchups always display as Road @ Home; spreads show from the favored team's perspective; live games show score plus quarter/time remaining; final games show score plus win margin.

Quick Check

Which leaderboard view shows one column per game, shaded by result?

What does the 'Pot. Pts' column on the Weekly Leaderboard represent?

What does the MGR badge indicate?

In Group view, what's the correct drill-down hierarchy?

On the Season-Long Leaderboard, what happens to a week that's been dropped via Drop Worst Week?

15

Auto-Picks & Missed Picks: CS Quick Reference

This is one of the most common support topics — "why did I get an auto-pick?" or "why did I score zero?" Here's the fast-reference version:

  • Auto-Picks are opt-in by the commissioner. If they're off, a missed pick simply scores zero — there is no fallback.
  • When Auto-Picks are on, team picks and Over/Under picks use two separate fallback strategies (e.g., "All Home Teams" for winners, "All Overs" for totals) — set either by the commissioner for everyone, or by each entrant individually.
  • Auto-picks only fill games the entrant hadn't already picked by the deadline — anything they did pick stands as-is.
  • A commissioner can cap the number of auto-pick events allowed all season (Maximum Auto Picks) and can forgive a small number of missed picks per week before they even count as an auto-pick event (Auto Pick Forgiveness).
The one-line answer for support

"Your missed picks were filled in using the auto-pick rule your commissioner set (or that you chose) — that's expected behavior once picks are on, not an error. If you'd rather not receive auto-picks, check with your commissioner about turning the feature off, since it applies contest-wide."

Quick Check

If Auto-Picks are off and an entrant misses a pick, what happens?

Do team picks and Over/Under picks share the same auto-pick fallback strategy?

What does Auto Pick Forgiveness actually forgive?

16

Entrants Manager

The Entrants manager is the commissioner's full roster view, with three sub-tabs: Entrants, Email entrants, and Pick log.

  • Filter pills across the top break the roster down by Active, Missing picks, Removed, Paused, entries with Notes, or No notes — plus a search bar to find a specific entrant.
  • The Download button (top right) exports the full entrant list.
  • Each row shows entered date, number of entries, how many picks are still missing, and an editable Notes field the commissioner can use for their own reference.
  • Email entrants lets a commissioner message specific entrants directly from within the contest.

The Pick log shows a running history of exactly when each entrant (or the commissioner, editing on their behalf) submitted picks for a given week — useful for confirming someone actually made picks, or seeing whether/when a pick was edited after the fact.

Live picks stay hidden

The Pick Log only reveals picks for weeks that have already locked — live, still-editable picks for the current week are intentionally hidden from the log to prevent any competitive advantage.

Quick Check

Which button exports the full entrant list?

Why does the Pick Log hide live, not-yet-locked picks?

What can a commissioner add to an individual entrant's row for their own reference?

17

Invites Manager

The Invites manager is where a commissioner brings new people into the contest.

  • Share link — generates a plain URL that can be copied and pasted anywhere (email, text, social media); anyone with the link can join.
  • Previous Splash entrants — quickly re-invite people who've played in one of the commissioner's past contests.
  • Followers — invite from the commissioner's Splash followers.
  • Import emails — bulk-add people by email address.

People added via any of those methods build up an Invite list on the right, which the commissioner reviews (optionally adding a custom message) before clicking Send invite. A Sent tab tracks everything already sent out.

Quick Check

Which invite method lets a commissioner paste a link anywhere, with no email needed?

Where can a commissioner see everything they've already sent?

18

FAQ & Glossary

FAQ

Can settings be changed after the contest starts?
Most settings can be adjusted at any time — leaderboard tiebreakers are explicitly editable mid-contest, and picksheet weekly customizations exist specifically to tweak individual weeks on the fly. Spreads, however, lock at the configured spread lock time and can't be edited after that point for a given week.

What happens if an entrant misses the pick deadline?
If Autopick is enabled, missed picks are automatically filled using the configured Team pick logic (e.g., all home teams) and Over/Under logic (e.g., random) — unless that entrant has hit their Maximum amount of auto picks cap, in which case the miss grades as a loss.

Can two entrants be tied at the end of the season?
The Season Leaderboard Tiebreakers list resolves this automatically, working through Points, Wins, and any additional tiebreakers a commissioner has added, in priority order.

Is Contest Vault required?
No — it's optional. Skipping it just means the commissioner manages entry fee collection outside of Splash themselves; using it waives the one-time service fee and lets LeagueSafe collect/distribute payments across all 50 states and Canada.

What's the difference between a Standard and a Confidence contest?
Standard scores each correct pick at its default value (1 point, unless weighted). Confidence has entrants rank every pick from most to least confident each week — a correctly-picked rank of 16 earns 16 points, a correctly-picked rank of 1 earns just 1. Missed picks consume the entrant's highest (or lowest, commissioner's choice) remaining values first. NFL Pick'em and NFL Confidence Pick'em are chosen as separate contest formats up front and can't be converted into each other later.

Can a contest use both Confidence and Best Pick?
No — they're mutually exclusive. Best Pick is a bonus feature layered on top of a Standard-format contest; Confidence contests don't have a Best Pick option.

Glossary

Straight upScoring mode where entrants pick outright winners, no spread involved.
Against the spread (ATS)Scoring mode where entrants pick which team covers a point spread.
PushA spread result that lands exactly on the line (a tie against the spread); requires a scoring rule.
HookA half-point spread line that makes pushes impossible.
ConfidenceAn alternate Format where entrants rank picks each week; correct picks score their assigned rank value.
Best PickA pick an entrant flags for bonus points if it hits, per the week's configured limit. A Standard-only add-on.
Fast PicksOne-click shortcuts that auto-fill an entrant's whole picksheet (e.g., All Home, All Favorites, Random).
AutopickAutomatic pick-filling for entrants who miss the weekly deadline.
SegmentA named group of consecutive weeks with its own standalone leaderboard.
Entrant GroupA sub-team of entrants (e.g., an office department) with its own group standings.
Tiebreaker gameThe single designated game each week where entrants predict the combined final score to help break ties.
Win MarginA leaderboard tiebreaker measuring cumulative margin of victory, spread cover amount, or O/U distance.
Contest VaultSplash's optional payment-collection tool (powered by LeagueSafe) shared across all contest formats.
Splash Sports internal training material — NFL Pick'em (Private Contests)
